fr:mainmatter.html:p: Actually, there are at least two different kinds of reasoning that get called reasoning by induction, and they are very much not the same! One is the kind that plays a role in the so-called "problem of induction"; that's not what I'm talking about here. The other is one that's sometimes called "mathematical induction" or "structural induction", and that's what we are doing.

Here it helps to be a bit more precise in a certain kind of ritualised way. (With all our symbols flying around, this is probably the class where you're most likely to accidentally summon a demon anyhow; let's lean into it!) The ritualised reasoning I want to introduce here is called reasoning by induction.

Actually, there are at least two different kinds of reasoning that get called reasoning by induction, and they are very much not the same! One is the kind that plays a role in the so-called "problem of induction"; that's not what I'm talking about here. The other is one that's sometimes called "mathematical induction" or "structural induction", and that's what we are doing.

The core of the idea is this: suppose we want to securely demonstrate that all formulas are such-and-such a way. The idea of induction doesn't depend on what we're trying to show about all formulas, so for now let's just say we want to show that all formulas are happy.

We can use what we know about how formulas are built up to systematically think about all formulas, and make sure we haven't left any of them out. Since there are more than six or seven formulas, we need some kind of system! The structure of formulas themselves is a very useful system much of the time, and it's what I'll use here.

We want to start, then, with the atomic sentences, showing that they are all happy. In practice, how we do this will depend on what it is we're really showing about all sentences; induction provides us no help here. But suppose we can do it: we've shown that all atomic sentences are happy.

What remains is all the compound sentences, and this is where induction really gives us its power. All we need to do here is to show that if all the components of a compound sentence are happy, then the compound sentence itself is also happy. That is, in showing that the compound sentence is happy, we can help ourselves to the assumption that all its components are happy.
This is important enough that it's handy to have a word for it:

A connective in a language preserves a property of sentences iff: whenever that connective is the main connective of a sentence, if all the components of that sentence have the property, then so does the sentence itself.
So what we're aiming to do is to show that all the connectives preserve the property of happiness.

Think about why this works: suppose it's true that all atomic sentences are happy, and that all the connectives preserve happiness. How, then, could there ever be a sentence that wasn't happy? How could it be built? We have to start from atomic sentences that are happy, and build up using connectives that can't ever bring us from happy sentences to not-happy ones. So any sentence we can manage to build in this way must be happy---but that's all the sentences!

If we can show the two parts of what we need, then:
that all atomic sentences are happy, and
that all connectives preserve happiness, then we've shown "by induction" that all sentences are happy.
